questions.question: What are the unique psychological benefits of solo sports compared to group exercise?

options.text: Solo sports have no psychological benefits compared to group exercise

Solo sports provide autonomy (self-directed practice), flow states (deep absorption), self-reliance (internal locus of control), and intrinsic motivation (mastery for its own sake) — all without social performance pressure
Solo sports are only for people who hate others
Everyone should always exercise in groups; solo sports are inferior

questions.explanation: Solo sports offer distinct benefits: autonomy (you control when, where, how hard — no external pressure), flow states (deep absorption reduces rumination), self-reliance (builds internal locus of control), and intrinsic motivation (pursuing mastery because it matters to YOU, not for external validation). These are particularly valuable for people with social anxiety, introverts, trauma survivors (need for control), or those seeking contemplative movement.

questions.question: What is a flow state, and how do solo sports trigger it?

options.text: Flow is a myth with no scientific basis

Flow is complete absorption in activity (timelessness, effortless focus, loss of self-consciousness) triggered by clear goals, immediate feedback, challenge-skill balance, and absence of social distraction
Flow only happens to elite athletes
Flow states are dangerous and should be avoided

questions.explanation: Flow is a psychological state characterized by complete absorption, timelessness, effortless focus, and loss of self-consciousness. Solo endurance sports (running, cycling, swimming, rock climbing) reliably trigger flow through: clear goals (run 5 miles), immediate feedback (pace, heart rate), challenge-skill balance (hard but not overwhelming), and no social distraction. Flow is therapeutic — it reduces rumination (can't worry while in flow) and enhances wellbeing post-experience.

questions.question: How do solo sports help build self-efficacy differently than group sports?

options.text: Solo sports don't build self-efficacy at all

In solo sports, success is undeniably yours (can't attribute to teammates or luck) — measurable progress (faster pace, heavier weight, harder climbing route) provides concrete proof of capability with full internal attribution
Self-efficacy only matters for professional athletes

questions.explanation: Solo sports force internal attribution for success — you can't credit teammates or luck when you run farther, lift heavier, or climb a harder route. The progress is measurable and undeniable, which counters depression's pattern of external attribution for success ('it doesn't count,' 'I got lucky'). Solo sports provide concrete proof: 'I did this. This is MY achievement.' This builds self-efficacy powerfully for those with low self-esteem or helplessness beliefs.

questions.question: For trauma survivors, why might solo sports be preferable to group settings initially?

options.text: Trauma survivors should never exercise at all
Solo sports provide total control and autonomy (you decide when to start, stop, how hard to push — no one touches you, no surprise commands) which helps re-establish safety and agency
Group exercise is always better for trauma recovery
Solo sports have no relevance to trauma recovery

questions.explanation: Trauma survivors often need control and autonomy in their environment. Group dynamics can feel unsafe (authority figures giving commands, physical proximity, unpredictability). Solo sports (walking, solo yoga at home, swimming in solo lanes, cycling) provide total control: you decide when to start, when to stop, how hard to push. No one touches you. No one commands you. This autonomy is therapeutic for re-establishing safety, agency, and bodily autonomy after trauma.

questions.question: Do you tend to thrive in group settings, or do you need solo time to recharge? How might this temperament inform your movement practice? (Both are valid — it's about knowing yourself.)
questions.explanation: This reflection applies the lesson's emphasis on matching modality to temperament and needs. Introverts often need solitude to recharge (group exercise can be socially depleting); they may prefer solo runs, home yoga, solo cycling. Extroverts may thrive on group energy and accountability. Some people need both: social workouts for motivation plus solo contemplative movement for restoration. There's no right answer — the lesson emphasizes self-knowledge over prescriptive rules.
